Samsung has once again, for the 4th year in a row, left the baseline S26 and S26+ with the same camera hardware, no clear info on whether USB‑C 3.2 is supported, and only tiny battery upgrades. Yet the prices keep going up, for what, exactly? Even the S26 Ultra barely changed this year. At this point, I’m skipping Samsung phones for the fourth year straight. Brands like Oppo, Vivo, and Xiaomi are offering far better camera systems, performance, and design for the money.

The S26 is a massive downgrade from the S25. It has become slightly bigger and heavier—an unnecessary change for what was a perfectly sized compact phone. Combined with those sharper edges, the ergonomics are jarring, making it much more uncomfortable to hold. ​We don't even need to mention moving back to Exynos from the S25's Snapdragon 8 Elite. When I compared them side-by-side, I noticed that even the display has been downgraded. It is dimmer than the S25 at full brightness, even though it doesn't even feature a privacy display. It’s likely the worst 'upgrade' we’ve seen from Samsung in years.

@eruno_ The thing is though, back then phones above 5.5 inch or above feel like phablets because they're wide and big, but a lot of the front is occupied by bezels. Now every new phone is pretty much bezel-less and taller (instead of wider) so the compact phones of today is almost similar to compact phones of old. The base iPhone 7 dimensions is 138.3 x 67.1 x 7.1 mm. Meanwhile, the base S26 is 149.6 x 71.7 x 7.2 mm. That's a 11mm increase on length while the screen is 1.6 inches larger. I have the base S25 and my dad still has the old iPhone 7, and barely noticed the physical size difference.

Excellent comparison as always. However, it is worth noting a significant variable regarding the Galaxy S26 Ultra. As a brand-new device, it is still in its 'learning usage patterns' phase. During the first week or two, the system is aggressively indexing files, synchronising accounts, and the AI is calibrating performance profiles based on user behaviour. This background activity typically leads to higher thermal output and accelerated battery drain. Since the S25 Ultra in this test is already fully optimised and 'settled', it holds a distinct advantage in efficiency and stability. A re-test in two weeks would likely yield more accurate results once the S26 Ultra’s background processes have stabilised.
